\boxed{B}
**Clinical Reasoning:** While minocycline has been explored in preclinical models as a potential neuroprotective agent due to its antioxidant properties, the retrieved evidence includes one reference citing *minocycline* as a "possible" neuroprotective pharmacologic agent in spinal cord injury research—this context is not directly applicable to ALS. No clinical trial data supporting the effectiveness of minocycline for ALS are mentioned anywhere in the provided sources. Given that the available literature focuses exclusively on established treatments (e.g., riluzole, edaravone, tofersen) or off-label uses (e.g., gabapentin, no benefit found), and omits any direct evidence linking minocycline to improved outcomes in ALS patients, we conclude that current clinical trial data do not support its use.
